segments.title: Project 58 and Gorshkov's ascent
segments.summary: Project 58 emerges as Sergei Gorshkov takes command, tying post-Stalin political change to a new Soviet appetite for major surface warships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the Kynda class beside Admiral Kuznetsov's 1956 removal and Gorshkov's rise to a remarkably long command. He contrasts Kuznetsov's reluctance toward big ships with Gorshkov's willingness to make them central to the fleet. The opening gives the class its political setting before turning to its missiles and machinery.

evidence.note: Introduces Project 58 and connects Gorshkov's rise to a different attitude toward large surface ships.

segments.title: Missile reach and maritime pressure
segments.summary: The Kynda's long-range anti-ship missiles made a Soviet cruiser a visible pressure point against task groups, convoys, ports, and alliance planning.
segments.body: The video contrasts Gorshkov's language of peaceful economic sea power with a warship carrying two salvos of large anti-ship missiles. Dr. Clarke argues that the missiles' range and conventional or nuclear warhead options widened the area an opponent had to consider at risk. Their value was therefore not limited to a single surface engagement: they could complicate convoy defence and maritime diplomacy.

evidence.note: Explains naval presence, the eight-plus-eight missile arrangement, and the Shaddock's range and warhead options.

segments.title: A cruiser hull built around missiles
segments.summary: This equipment tour shows how the Kynda combined a cruiser-sized hull and long-range anti-ship missiles with modest air defence, guns, torpedoes, and ASW weapons.
segments.body: At roughly 4,400 tons standard, the class was markedly larger than contemporary Soviet destroyers and had the endurance and speed expected of an ocean-going surface combatant. Its two quadruple anti-ship launchers dominated the design, but the ship also carried a Volna air-defence system, twin 76 mm guns, RBU-6000 launchers, torpedoes, and a helicopter deck. Dr. Clarke flags that published figures can differ, so the moment is most useful for understanding the overall balance of capabilities rather than a single definitive fit list.

evidence.note: Lists displacement, machinery, range, anti-ship and anti-air missiles, guns, ASW weapons, torpedoes, and helipad.

segments.title: Classification, electronics, and slow reloads
segments.summary: Kynda's cruiser-like hull, large missile tubes, distinctive radar structure, and awkward reload process show why the class was more than a renamed destroyer.
segments.body: The account follows the class through Soviet and American labels, while stressing that its hull form and firepower fitted a cruiser more closely than a destroyer. Large missile tubes swung outward to fire, with reloads stored in the superstructure, but the reload cycle was slow and cumbersome. Changes to radar fits also mattered because missile control depended on the associated control systems and data links, not just on a new antenna.

evidence.note: Covers class labels, hull form, missile-launcher arrangement, radar fits, reload limitations, and fit differences.

segments.title: Gorshkov's design laboratory at Leningrad
segments.summary: The four ships were an early opportunity to test Gorshkov's ideas before later Soviet cruiser designs carried those lessons forward.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes the class as an experimental opening for a new commander: a way to discover what could be built, with what technology, and for which missions. The ships entered service quickly from the Zhdanov yard in Leningrad, a rebuilt and long-lived centre for large Soviet surface-combatant construction. Their differing details matter because they reveal a navy learning while it built rather than simply repeating a settled design.

evidence.note: Describes the class as a test of Gorshkov's ideas and traces construction at the Zhdanov yard.

segments.title: A visible cruiser as political pressure
segments.summary: A Kynda could be more diplomatically useful than a hidden missile submarine because its conspicuous weapons advertised a threat without immediately creating a crisis.
segments.body: The video distinguishes the deterrent effect of a surfaced missile cruiser from that of a submarine near an opponent's coast. Its missiles, air-defence weapons, ASW launchers, torpedoes, and guns created a comprehensible but formidable image for Cold War observers. Even a small pairing with another Soviet ship could compel an opponent to devote much larger forces to tracking and countering it.

evidence.note: Contrasts visible surface ships with submarines and explains the pressure a Kynda-led surface group could impose.

segments.title: Iterating air defence and machinery
segments.summary: The class illustrates how early Soviet missile and steam-plant choices were refined ship by ship rather than frozen in a single mature design.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the likely V-600 and V-601 versions of the Volna missile, using their development dates and ranges to explain why individual ships may not have carried identical fits. He also describes the high-pressure boiler plant as capable but temperamental, with a design lineage that suggests German influence. The uncertainty is part of the lesson: early guided-missile cruisers were still working out their technology and support systems.

evidence.note: Discusses uncertain Volna missile fits, their range differences, and the class's high-pressure boiler experience.

segments.title: Kynda's blue-water raider rationale
segments.summary: Kynda becomes the starting point for a blue-water Soviet surface force designed to distract, disperse, and pressure the Atlantic supply system rather than win alone.
segments.body: The closing argument treats the class as a formative step toward Gorshkov's ocean-going fleet, carrying the Soviet desire for security, reach, and disruption beyond home waters. Dr. Clarke does not present surface raiders as a war-winning substitute for a stronger fleet. Instead, he explains how they could divide enemy attention and make the convoy flow from North America to Europe harder to protect at the decisive time.

evidence.note: Presents Kynda as a genesis point for blue-water reach and explains the raider strategy against Atlantic reinforcement.
